subsystem: FontForge SFD (Spline Font Database) parser. SFD_GetFontMetaData() is the per-token dispatcher inside FontForge's native-format loader (sfd.c). It is called once per keyword line of an .sfd font file: given the already-read token string tok, it reads the remainder of that line/record from the FILE* sfd and stores the decoded value into the SplineFont *sf being built. It handles font names, PostScript/OS2 metrics, panose, vendor id, layers, kerning classes, mark-attachment classes/sets, lookups, contextual/state-machine subtables, glyph groups, and TrueType tables.
provenance: Identified as FontForge by the function name SFD_GetFontMetaData, the SplineFont/KernClass/KernClass1/FPST/FPST1/ASM/ASM1/OTLookup/LayerInfo/DeviceTable types, the pfminfo.* PostScript/OS2 metric field names, the SFDReadUTF7Str / SFDReadDeviceTable / SFDParseBase / SFDParseChainContext / SFDParseStateMachine helpers, chunkalloc(), IError()/LogError()/_() i18n, and the 'sfd file' / 'sfd_version' vocabulary. The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_validation_0331_fixed */' marks it as a curated (purportedly patched) sample. SFD is FontForge's native ASCII/UTF-7 font-source format.
trust_boundary: The FILE *sfd is an attacker-controlled font source file: a user (or a service processing uploaded/downloaded fonts) opens an untrusted .sfd, and every byte read via geteol/getint/getsint/getreal/getlonglong/gethex/gethexints/nlgetc/fread/SFDReadUTF7Str comes straight off that file with no authentication. All integer counts (mark_class_cnt, mark_set_cnt, first_cnt, second_cnt, layer_cnt_tmp, layer, per-string temp lengths) and all string bytes are therefore hostile: an SFD file can declare any 32-bit count and any string length. tok is the caller-supplied token buffer; sf is a heap object being populated. Nothing here validates that declared counts/lengths are non-negative, in range, or consistent with the file's actual size.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The dispatcher takes the open font file sfd, the keyword token tok that the caller already matched, the SplineFont sf it fills in, and a per-parse scratch/state object d (holding running 'last' pointers for linked lists such as lastkc/lastfp/lastsm/last_base). Local val is a fixed 2000-byte stack buffer reused as the destination for line-oriented string reads. ch/i/old/kc are loop and temporary variables. The function returns bool: true if it recognized and consumed the token, false otherwise.

A NULL-hardening shim: if the caller passes d==NULL, the function substitutes a function-local static instance (initialized once, guarded by my_static_d_is_virgin) so later code can dereference d unconditionally. This is the defensive change that gives the sample its '_fixed' suffix; the original defect class was a NULL dereference / lifetime issue on d. The static makes the routine non-reentrant and not thread-safe, but removes the NULL-deref on d.

The core structure is a long strmatch() if/else-if ladder, one arm per SFD keyword. The simple string arms (FontName/FullName/FamilyName/DefaultBaseFilename/Version/FONDName/XUID) call geteol(sfd,val) to read the rest of the line into the 2000-byte val buffer, then copy() it onto a heap string field of sf. geteol is the sole gatekeeper of the val[2000] bound: the arms themselves pass no size.

Other string arms use different readers: getprotectedname (into val), getquotedeol (returns a fresh heap buffer), and SFDReadUTF7Str (decodes FontForge's UTF-7 string encoding into a fresh heap buffer). The Comments arm reads a quoted line, re-encodes Latin-1 to UTF-8, and frees the temporary. These helpers own their own bounds/allocation; the string content is fully attacker-controlled but the arms are just plumbing.

The bulk of the ladder is scalar metric parsing: getreal into double fields (italicangle, strokewidth, upos, uwidth, ufo_ascent/descent), getlonglong into 64-bit time fields, getint/getsint into int/int16 pfminfo and sf fields (ascent/descent, hhead/os2 metrics, subsuper offsets, weight/width, flags). Each writes one fixed-width scalar to a named struct member. The values are attacker-chosen but land in appropriately sized lvalues, so these arms are not memory-unsafe in themselves.

The Panose arm reads exactly ten integers into pfminfo.panose[0..9] with a compile-time constant loop bound of 10, matching the OpenType PANOSE array size. The count is not attacker-controlled, so there is no index overrun here.

OS2Vendor skips whitespace then writes exactly four bytes into the 4-byte os2_vendor tag plus consumes a trailing byte. OS2CodePages/OS2UnicodeRanges call gethexints with fixed element counts (2 and 4) into the correspondingly sized codepages/unicoderanges arrays. All destinations use constant, in-range indices/counts, so these arms are bounded.

LayerCount reads a requested layer count layer_cnt_tmp from the file and, if it exceeds the two mandatory layers, is supposed to grow sf->layers to hold layer_cnt_tmp entries. Note that the realloc size and the memset size both use the OLD sf->layer_cnt, while only sf->layer_cnt is then bumped to layer_cnt_tmp. The array is therefore resized to the old capacity but the recorded count is enlarged.

The Layer arm reads a layer index layer from the file, grows sf->layers to layer+1 entries when layer>=layer_cnt, then indexes sf->layers[layer] to set order2, background, name (an SFDReadUTF7Str), and optionally ufo_path. The index and the growth arithmetic are entirely driven by the untrusted integer layer. This is the primary array-indexing surface of the function.

MarkAttachClasses reads a count mark_class_cnt, allocates two pointer arrays of that many char* via malloc, then loops reading each class: a UTF-7 name, an integer length temp, a malloc(temp+1) buffer whose byte [temp] is immediately NUL-terminated, and a fread of temp bytes. Both the outer count and every per-class temp length are unvalidated file integers, and none of the malloc return values are checked. MarkAttachSets (lines 579-595) is the identical pattern for mark_set_cnt/mark_sets/mark_set_names.

The KernClass family (KernClass/VKernClass and versions 2/3) allocates a KernClass, reads first_cnt and second_cnt from the file, then allocates firsts/seconds pointer arrays and the offsets/adjusts matrices sized first_cnt*second_cnt. The subsequent loops (lines 646-688) fill firsts[i]/seconds[i] with malloc(temp+1)+fread strings (same pattern as MarkAttach) and iterate i<first_cnt*second_cnt to read every offset and device table. The two counts and the product are attacker-controlled and computed in int before being handed to calloc.

The XUID arm is unusual: it calls geteol into the caller's tok buffer (not the local val), then copies it. The Lookup arm enforces sfd_version>=2 (exit(1) otherwise), chunk-allocates an OTLookup, reads its type/flags/name, links it onto the gsub or gpos list via d->lastsotl/lastpotl, and delegates the body to SFDParseLookup. Version-mismatch is treated as fatal rather than a graceful error.

The remaining arms (FPST contextual/chaining subtables, Mac state machines, Group/GroupKern/GroupVKern glyph classes, MacFeat, TtfTable/TtTable/ShortTable) chunk/calloc a record, link it onto the appropriate d->last* list, and delegate the actual field parsing to helpers (SFDParseChainContext/SFDParseStateMachine/SFDParseMacFeatures/SFDGetTtfTable/...). Version consistency is checked with the (sf->sfd_version<2)!=old pattern that exit(1)s on mismatch. If no arm matched, the function returns false; otherwise true.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: layer is a signed int read directly from the file with no lower-bound or upper-bound check. Negative index: a NEGATIVE layer (e.g. -1) fails the layer>=sf->layer_cnt growth test (a negative is < layer_cnt), so no realloc happens and the code writes sf->layers[-1].order2 / .background / .name — an out-of-bounds write before the allocation, corrupting heap metadata. Huge index: a value near INT_MAX makes (layer+1)*sizeof(LayerInfo) overflow the size_t/int computation to a small number, so realloc returns a tiny buffer while sf->layer_cnt is set to layer+1; the subsequent sf->layers[layer] writes and the memset of ((layer+1)-sf->layer_cnt) elements run far past the allocation. The implementation must reject layer<0 and bound layer+1 against a sane maximum (and use size_t multiplication with overflow checking) before allocating or indexing. This is the classic SFD_GetFontMetaData out-of-bounds font-parsing bug.

The reallocation size uses the OLD sf->layer_cnt, not the requested layer_cnt_tmp: sf->layers is reallocated to sf->layer_cnt*sizeof(LayerInfo) (no growth), yet sf->layer_cnt is then assigned the larger layer_cnt_tmp. From this point the array's recorded capacity exceeds its real capacity, so any later code that iterates or indexes 0..layer_cnt-1 (including a later Layer: arm that skips its own grow branch because layer<layer_cnt) reads and writes past the end of the allocation. The invariant is that the allocation size and sf->layer_cnt must be derived from the SAME (validated) count; here they diverge, giving a heap buffer overflow driven purely by the file's declared LayerCount.

mark_class_cnt is an unvalidated file int. mark_class_cnt*sizeof(char*) can integer-overflow (CWE-190) to a small allocation while the i<mark_class_cnt loop writes far beyond it; a negative or zero count makes malloc(0)/huge and the immediate sf->mark_classes[0]=NULL store touches an under-sized/NULL buffer. Neither malloc is NULL-checked, so on failure the very next dereference is a NULL write (CWE-476). Per element, temp is an unvalidated length: temp==-1 makes malloc(0) then mark_classes[i][-1]='\0' (OOB write) and fread(...,(size_t)-1,...) attempts an enormous read; a large temp with malloc(temp+1) can wrap temp+1 to 0. The implementation must reject negative/oversized counts and lengths, check every malloc, and use overflow-safe sizing. MarkAttachSets (581-593) has the identical defect on mark_set_cnt.

first_cnt and second_cnt are unvalidated file ints. The product first_cnt*second_cnt is computed in int BEFORE being passed to calloc, so it can overflow (CWE-190) to a small value even though calloc's own count*size guard would catch an honest large request; the offsets/adjusts matrices are then under-allocated while the later loop for(i=0;i<first_cnt*second_cnt;++i) writes kc->offsets[i]/kc->adjusts[i] past the end. Negative counts pass into calloc as huge size_t (NULL, unchecked) or make the loops misbehave. The per-string malloc(temp+1)+kc->firsts[i][temp]='\0'+fread(...,temp,...) pattern repeats the MarkAttach length bug for firsts/seconds. Safe code must validate both counts, compute the product in a wide type with overflow checking, check every calloc/malloc, and bound each temp length against remaining input.

val is a fixed char[2000] on the stack and every string arm (FontName/FullName/FamilyName/DefaultBaseFilename/Weight/Version/FONDName) reads an arbitrarily long file line into it via geteol/getprotectedname with no length argument. If geteol does not internally clamp to 2000 bytes, an attacker-supplied over-long line produces a classic stack buffer overflow (CWE-120/CWE-787) with return-address corruption. This is a latent finding: the safety depends entirely on geteol's implementation (not shown). The invariant is that geteol MUST bound its write to the caller's buffer size; because the call site passes no size, that contract is invisible and must be confirmed.

The XUID arm reads a whole file line into tok, the CALLER's token buffer, rather than the local val[2000]. The size of tok is not visible here and is presumably sized only to hold a keyword token, so reading an unbounded end-of-line into it can overflow whatever buffer the caller supplied (CWE-120/CWE-787). This is distinct from the val overflow because the destination is an out-parameter of unknown, likely smaller, size. Confirm the caller's tok allocation and that geteol clamps to it.

Both layer reallocations (lines 372 and 383) use the p = realloc(p, n) anti-pattern: on allocation failure realloc returns NULL, the only pointer to the old block is overwritten with NULL (memory leak, CWE-401) and the immediately following memset(sf->layers+... ) / sf->layers[layer] dereferences NULL (CWE-476). The result must be assigned to a temporary and checked before overwriting sf->layers. The same unchecked-return concern applies to every malloc/calloc in the MarkAttach and KernClass arms.

The NULL-d shim removes the NULL dereference but introduces shared mutable state: the function-local statics my_static_d/my_static_d_is_virgin mean two concurrent parses (or nested calls) that pass d==NULL share one state object, so the running 'last' list pointers (lastkc/lastfp/lastsm/last_base/...) can be crossed between fonts. That is a concurrency/state-confusion hazard (data race, and potentially a stale pointer reused across fonts leading to use-after-free) rather than a spatial overflow. Low severity relative to the layer/count bugs, but worth noting because it is the sample's deliberate change; the invariant is that per-parse state must not be shared across invocations.

summary: This FontForge SFD metadata dispatcher parses a fully attacker-controlled font-source file and trusts every integer count and length it reads. The strongest issues are heap/stack memory-safety bugs in the Layer/LayerCount handling (unbounded, unsigned/overflowing, and negative layer indices; a realloc that grows the recorded count without growing the buffer) and in the MarkAttach/KernClass count-and-length loops (int-overflowing size arithmetic, unchecked allocations, and negative per-string lengths driving OOB writes and giant freads). These correspond to the well-known SFD_GetFontMetaData out-of-bounds parsing vulnerability (CVE-2020-5395). The scalar-metric arms are benign; the NULL-d static shim fixes a NULL deref but adds a shared-state hazard.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char val[2000]; (fixed 2000-byte stack buffer, target of geteol/getprotectedname)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Filled by unbounded line readers with no size argument
Shared mutable per-parse state when d==NULL: non-reentrant, cross-font pointer confusion
Unbounded read into val[2000]
Unbounded read into caller's tok buffer of unknown size
Same val[2000] unbounded-fill contract as geteol
Return freshly allocated heap strings; bounds/allocation owned inside the (not-shown) helper

reviewed_anchors.anchor: getreal/getint/getsint/getlonglong into sf/pfminfo scalar fields (metric arms)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker value written to a single fixed-width scalar lvalue; not a buffer
Compile-time constant bound of 10 matches array size
Four constant, in-range indices into a 4-byte tag
Fixed element counts matching destination array sizes
Realloc sized from old count but recorded count enlarged -> under-allocated array
Signed, unbounded index: negative OOB and (layer+1)*size overflow
Unvalidated count/length, int-overflow sizing, unchecked malloc, negative-temp OOB
Identical count/length/allocation defect as MarkAttachClasses
int-product overflow into calloc, unchecked allocs, negative per-string length
p=realloc(p,n) loses pointer and NULL-derefs on failure
Fatal-on-mismatch; DoS-via-exit only, not memory unsafe; body delegated to SFDParseLookup
Fixed-size chunkalloc; parsing delegated to helpers not shown
Single-element allocation of fixed size; string content owned by SFDReadUTF7Str
NULL-guarded linked-list append; frees bs only when last_base is NULL
Delegated to helpers; d->lastttf lifetime not observable here
Type-pun write to a same-width scalar; not a spatial overflow

reviewed_anchors.anchor: final else return false / return true (821-828)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Control-flow result only

findings.title: Out-of-bounds write via unvalidated Layer index (negative and integer-overflow)
findings.invariant: layer must be validated as 0 <= layer < sane_max before use, and (layer+1)*sizeof(LayerInfo) must be computed in a wide type with overflow checking before realloc/index.
findings.failure_mode: A negative layer bypasses the layer>=sf->layer_cnt grow branch and writes sf->layers[layer] below the buffer; a layer near INT_MAX overflows (layer+1)*sizeof(LayerInfo) to a tiny realloc while sf->layer_cnt becomes layer+1, so the indexed writes and the memset run far past the allocation. Result: attacker-controlled heap corruption from a crafted .sfd, leading to code execution.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether getint can yield a negative/huge value and whether any bound check on layer exists before sf->layers[layer] is written.

findings.title: LayerCount reallocates to old count but inflates layer_cnt (heap overflow)
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow (allocation size / recorded count mismatch)
findings.invariant: The realloc size and the value stored into sf->layer_cnt must be derived from the SAME validated count (layer_cnt_tmp), so capacity always covers the recorded element count.
findings.failure_mode: realloc grows sf->layers only to the OLD sf->layer_cnt entries, yet sf->layer_cnt is then set to the larger layer_cnt_tmp. Every later access to sf->layers[0..layer_cnt-1] (e.g. a subsequent Layer: whose index is < the inflated count and so skips its own grow branch) reads/writes past the real allocation. A crafted LayerCount value yields a controllable heap overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the realloc argument should be layer_cnt_tmp*sizeof(LayerInfo); check all consumers that iterate/index up to sf->layer_cnt.

findings.title: MarkAttachClasses/Sets: overflowing count and negative string length drive OOB writes
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow / unchecked allocation leading to out-of-bounds write
findings.invariant: mark_class_cnt and each per-class temp must be validated non-negative and bounded; count*sizeof and temp+1 must not overflow; every malloc return must be NULL-checked before use.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted mark_class_cnt overflows cnt*sizeof(char*) to a small allocation while the i<cnt loop writes past it; a negative count makes malloc huge/NULL and the mark_classes[0]=NULL store hits an under-sized/NULL buffer (CWE-476). Per class, temp==-1 gives malloc(0) then mark_classes[i][-1]='\0' (OOB write) and fread with size (size_t)-1; a large temp wraps temp+1 to 0. MarkAttachSets (581-593) is identical for mark_set_cnt.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether counts/lengths are validated and whether malloc results are checked before dereference.

findings.title: KernClass matrix sizing overflows (first_cnt*second_cnt computed in int)
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in size arithmetic leading to undersized buffer and OOB write
findings.invariant: first_cnt and second_cnt must be validated non-negative and their product computed in a wide (size_t) type with overflow detection before calloc; per-string temp lengths must be bounded and allocations NULL-checked.
findings.failure_mode: first_cnt*second_cnt is evaluated as int; with attacker counts it overflows to a small value, so kc->offsets/kc->adjusts are undersized while the later for(i=0;i<first_cnt*second_cnt;++i) loop writes kc->offsets[i]/kc->adjusts[i] past the end (calloc's own overflow guard is bypassed because the product is already truncated before the call). Negative counts pass to calloc as huge size_t returning NULL (unchecked). The kc->firsts[i]=malloc(temp+1); [temp]='\0'; fread pattern repeats the negative/overflow length bug per string.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether first_cnt/second_cnt are range-checked and the product is overflow-safe; whether calloc/malloc returns are checked.

findings.title: Unbounded line read into fixed 2000-byte stack buffer (latent stack overflow)
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (copy without size check)
findings.invariant: geteol/getprotectedname must clamp their writes to the caller's buffer size; since the call sites pass no size, the reader itself must enforce the 2000-byte bound.
findings.failure_mode: If geteol/getprotectedname copy an entire file line into val without a 2000-byte limit, an over-long FontName/Weight/Version/etc. line overflows the stack buffer and can overwrite the return address (CWE-787). Latent because the bound lives in the not-shown reader.
findings.what_to_confirm: The geteol/getprotectedname implementations: do they bound-check against the destination size?

findings.title: XUID reads a full line into the caller's token buffer of unknown size
findings.bug_class: Buffer overflow into out-parameter (copy without size check)
findings.invariant: tok must be at least as large as the longest line geteol can produce, or geteol must clamp to tok's size; the size of tok must be known to the reader.
findings.failure_mode: Unlike other arms that use val[2000], the XUID arm reads an unbounded end-of-line into the caller's tok buffer, which is typically sized only for a keyword token. A long XUID line overflows whatever the caller allocated for tok.
findings.what_to_confirm: The caller's allocation size for tok and whether geteol clamps to it.

findings.title: realloc return assigned to the same pointer (leak + NULL deref on failure)
findings.invariant: realloc result must be assigned to a temporary and NULL-checked before overwriting sf->layers; malloc/calloc results must be checked before dereference.
findings.failure_mode: On allocation failure realloc returns NULL, sf->layers is overwritten with NULL (the old block leaks, CWE-401) and the immediately following memset/sf->layers[layer] writes dereference NULL and crash. The same unchecked-return risk applies to the LayerCount realloc (372) and the MarkAttach/KernClass mallocs. An attacker can force this with a huge declared count.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any allocation-failure handling exists on these realloc/malloc/calloc sites.

findings.title: NULL-d shim introduces shared function-static state (non-reentrant / cross-font pointer reuse)
findings.anchor: static SFD_GetFontMetaDataData my_static_d; static int my_static_d_is_virgin = 1;
findings.bug_class: Shared mutable state / stale pointer reuse (potential use-after-free)
findings.invariant: Per-parse state (the running last* list pointers) must be private to each font-parsing session and never shared across invocations or threads.
findings.failure_mode: When callers pass d==NULL, all such calls share one function-static my_static_d whose lastkc/lastfp/lastsm/last_base pointers persist across different fonts and are never reset. A pointer captured while parsing one font (or freed when that font is discarded) can be reused or linked while parsing another, and concurrent/nested parses race on the shared object -> stale-pointer use / use-after-free. This is the deliberate change that trades a NULL-deref for a lifetime hazard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any caller actually passes d==NULL, and whether my_static_d's list pointers are reset between fonts.

audit_checklist: Layer: reject layer<0 and bound layer+1; compute (layer+1)*sizeof(LayerInfo) in size_t with overflow check before realloc and before indexing sf->layers[layer].
LayerCount: change the realloc size to layer_cnt_tmp*sizeof(LayerInfo) so allocation capacity matches the sf->layer_cnt written afterward.

MarkAttachClasses/MarkAttachSets: validate mark_class_cnt/mark_set_cnt non-negative and bounded, use overflow-safe cnt*sizeof, NULL-check both mallocs, and reject negative/oversized per-class temp before malloc(temp+1)/fread.
KernClass: validate first_cnt/second_cnt, compute first_cnt*second_cnt in size_t with overflow detection before calloc, NULL-check allocations, and bound each per-string temp length.
Confirm geteol/getprotectedname clamp to the destination size (val[2000]); the XUID arm's geteol into tok must be bounded to the caller's tok allocation.
Replace every p = realloc(p, n) with a checked temporary; NULL-check all malloc/calloc results before first dereference.
Reconsider the my_static_d function-static: ensure per-parse list state is not shared across fonts or threads when d==NULL.
Audit the delegated helpers (SFDReadUTF7Str, SFDParseLookup, SFDParseChainContext, SFDParseStateMachine, SFDReadDeviceTable) for the same unvalidated-length/count patterns.
